title: Acute Gouty Arthritis

patient.name: James Thompson
patient.persona: business executive, heavy red meat eater, daily alcohol, frustrated by recurrent attacks

chief_complaint: My big toe is on fire! I woke up at 3am with the worst pain I've ever felt in my left big toe. I can't even bear the weight of a sheet on it. I have a presentation tomorrow and I can't walk!

physical_exam.General: Uncomfortable, holding left foot, unable to bear weight
physical_exam.Left Foot: First MTP joint: Swollen, erythematous, exquisitely tender to touch. Warmth over the joint. Skin shiny, peeling. No ulceration or drainage. Range of motion severely limited by pain.
physical_exam.Other Joints: No other joints affected. No tophi visible. No olecranon bursitis.

hidden_data.arthrocentesis: Synovial fluid analysis: WBC 25,000/µL (85% neutrophils). Negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals under polarized light microscopy. Gram stain: No organisms. Culture pending.
hidden_data.uric_acid: Serum uric acid 9.8 mg/dL (normal <7.0). Significantly elevated.
hidden_data.cmp: Creatinine 1.2, BUN 18, Glucose 105, normal electrolytes. Normal LFTs.
hidden_data.xray_foot: Soft tissue swelling around left 1st MTP joint. No erosions. No chondrocalcinosis. Joint space preserved. Chronic findings: Small erosion with overhanging edge (classic gouty erosion).

correct_diagnosis: Acute Gouty Arthritis of Left First Metatarsophalangeal Joint (Podagra)
key_learning_points: Gout = monosodium urate crystal deposition in joints due to hyperuricemia. Classic presentation: Acute-onset severe monoarticular arthritis, often involving first MTP (podagra)
Definitive diagnosis: Synovial fluid analysis with negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals on polarized light microscopy
Acute treatment: NSAIDs (first-line), Colchicine (low-dose 1.2mg then 0.6mg 1h later), or Corticosteroids
Urate-lowering therapy (Allopurinol/Febuxostat): Start AFTER acute flare resolves. Target uric acid <6.0 mg/dL
Dietary triggers: Red meat, shellfish, alcohol (especially beer), fructose. Diuretics increase uric acid

patient_responses.pain: It's excruciating - 10 out of 10. I can't put any weight on it. Even the bedsheet touching it is agony.
patient_responses.onset: I had a big steak dinner with clients last night, a few glasses of red wine and whiskey. I woke up at 3am with this pain. This has happened before - usually once or twice a year for the past 3 years.
patient_responses.previous_attacks: Usually my big toe. Once it was my ankle. Each attack lasts about a week then goes away on its own.
patient_responses.diet: I travel a lot for work so I eat at steakhouses regularly. I drink whiskey most evenings and wine with dinner.
patient_responses.medications: I take hydrochlorothiazide for blood pressure. That's it.

hints.history: Sudden-onset severe monoarticular arthritis in a middle-aged man with dietary triggers = gout until proven otherwise
hints.examination: Podagra (1st MTP) is classic. Look for tophi. Check for signs of infection to rule out septic arthritis
hints.labs: Arthrocentesis is diagnostic gold standard. Serum uric acid may be normal during acute attack
hints.imaging: X-ray: May show erosions with overhanging edges (chronic finding)

feedback.correct_message: Excellent! You correctly diagnosed acute gouty arthritis based on the classic presentation and negatively birefringent crystals on synovial fluid analysis.
feedback.partially_correct_message: Good recognition of gout! However, arthrocentesis is critical to rule out septic arthritis which can coexist or mimic gout.
feedback.incorrect_message: The classic presentation with synovial fluid showing negatively birefringent crystals is definitive gout. Acute management with NSAIDs/Colchicine/Steroids and long-term urate-lowering therapy are indicated.
clinical_pearls: Low-dose colchicine is as effective as high-dose with significantly fewer GI side effects
NEVER start allopurinol during an acute attack - wait 2-4 weeks after resolution
Serum uric acid can be NORMAL during an acute attack

arthrocentesis.comment: Negatively birefringent crystals = gout. Gram stain negative rules out septic arthritis

feedback.attending_note: Acute management: Naproxen 500mg BID or Colchicine low-dose. Prednisone 30-40mg daily if NSAIDs contraindicated. Consider changing HCTZ to Losartan. After flare resolves: Start Allopurinol 100mg daily, titrate to uric acid <6.0. Colchicine prophylaxis ×6 months. Dietary counseling: Reduce red meat, alcohol, fructose. Weight loss goal: 10% body weight.
